ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for tomato chutney as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for condiment subclasses is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tomato chutney is approximately 1 tablespoon (15 g), used as a condiment alongside rice, bread, or snacks.

nutrient_source.title: Chutney, tomato — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chutney (tomato), cr_id F14080008, confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tomato-based products and cooked tomato preparations — analogous estimate based on tomato (GI ~35) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for tomato chutney in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous food: cooked tomato / tomato-based products (GI ~35) from the University of Sydney GI Database. High dietary fibre content (5.54 g/100g) further supports a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (35 × 7.16 × 0.15) / 100 ≈ 0.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 248 mg/100g — borderline elevated for a condiment; potassium ~280 mg/100g and dietary fibre 5.54 g/100g provide partial offset
hypertension.guidance: Limit tomato chutney to 1–2 tablespoons (15–30 g) per meal to keep sodium intake below 75–150 mg per serving; choose low-salt preparation variants where available and pair with high-potassium foods to support blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~35), low carbohydrate (7.16 g/100g), high dietary fibre (5.54 g/100g) — glycemic load per 15 g serving is approximately 0.4 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tomato chutney is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used as a condiment in standard 15 g portions; the high fibre content slows glucose absorption and the low glycemic load minimises postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100g), high dietary fibre (5.54 g/100g), lycopene-rich tomato base; sodium at 248 mg/100g warrants portion control
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tomato chutney provides cardioprotective lycopene and dietary fibre in small condiment servings; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 15 g to keep sodium contribution below 40 mg and avoid high-salt commercial preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato and chili are nightshade vegetables containing solanine, which may trigger or exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables (tomato, chili, eggplant, pepper) should avoid tomato chutney or trial elimination and monitor joint symptoms before reintroducing.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato chutney is a tangy, spiced condiment made by slow-cooking ripe tomatoes with onions, garlic, ginger, chili, and a blend of spices such as cumin, coriander, and mustard seeds, often finished with fresh herbs. The result is a thick, jammy sauce with a balance of sweet, sour, and savoury flavours and a soft, slightly chunky texture. It is a staple condiment across South Asian cuisines — particularly in Indian, Sri Lankan, and South African cooking — and is widely used in Singapore as an accompaniment to snacks, rice dishes, and flatbreads. Nutritionally, tomato chutney provides 77 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (2.82 g), moderate fat (4.15 g), low carbohydrate (7.16 g), and notably high dietary fibre (5.54 g/100g). Sodium is 248 mg/100g, which is borderline elevated for a condiment and warrants portion control in hypertensive individuals. The high fibre content and low glycemic index (~35) make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard condiment portions. Tomatoes contribute lycopene, a carotenoid antioxidant associated with cardiovascular protection. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution due to the nightshade content (tomato and chili), which may aggravate solanine-sensitive joint inflammation. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080008, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on cooked tomato products. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100g and potassium at 280 mg/100g from USDA FoodData Central closest equivalent (tomato-based condiment); these secondary values are documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.6 g/100g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tomato-based condiment/chutney (closest approved-source equivalent); not directly reported in HPB Foodo entry. 2. Potassium (280 mg/100g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy tomato-based condiment data; HPB Foodo entry does not report potassium. 3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific tomato chutney preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. Cholesterol set to 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients). 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on cooked tomato / tomato-based products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI entry exists for tomato chutney. Glycemic load per 15 g serving calculated as (35 × 7.16 × 0.15) / 100 = 0.38, rounded to 0.4 g. 6. This food is classified under 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' in the output to better reflect its functional role as a condiment; the input classified it under 'Vegetables / Vegetable-based dishes'. 7. Multilingual coverage: Tamil and Hindi names provided as this is a South Asian condiment widely used in Indian cuisine. Chinese and Malay names are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean dish requiring the full SG multilingual matrix; the food is of South Asian origin.
